senses.definition: the quality of being understandable or capable of being understood.
examples: The teacher explained the concept with great clarity, increasing the overall prehensibility of the material.

senses.definition: capable of being understood; intelligible.
examples: The instructions are prehensible once you read them slowly.
Her handwriting is barely prehensible to anyone but herself.

senses.definition: capable of grasping or holding, especially by use of a limb or organ that can grasp.
examples: The monkey has a prehensile tail that it uses to grip branches.
Some creatures have prehensile tongues or appendages for feeding.

senses.definition: plural of prehensility; the capacities or instances of being prehensile.
examples: Different species exhibit varying prehensilities.
The study compared edge cases of prehensilities in mammals.

senses.definition: the state or quality of being prehensile; the ability to grasp.
examples: The prehensility of the octopus is remarkable.
Researchers tested the limits of prehensility in different species.

senses.definition: the act of grasping or understanding; comprehension or perception.
examples: Her prehension of the text was quick and accurate.
The film requires careful prehension of its themes.

senses.definition: plural of prehension; instances or acts of grasping or understanding.
examples: The biologist noted several unusual prehensions among specimens.
Analysts tracked the prehensons of the language learners.

senses.definition: comprehensive; including all or much; broad in scope.
examples: A comprehensive study was published on the topic.
The report provides comprehensive coverage of the issue.

senses.definition: the quality of being comprehensive; including or dealing with all or nearly all elements or aspects.
examples: The guide's comprehensiveness makes it a valuable resource.
There is room to improve the comprehensiveness of the documentation.

senses.definition: one who prehends; a holder or restrainer in certain mechanical or legal senses; a person who captures or seizes.
examples: The legal preensor acted to secure evidence.
In anatomy, a preensor muscle performs a grasping action.

senses.definition: relating to a preensor; capable of grasping or restraining.
examples: The prehensorial apparatus aids the creature in climbing.
Anatomists describe the prehensorial muscles in detail.

senses.definition: plural of preensor; devices or persons that prehend or seize.
examples: The cage includes multiple prehensors to secure the net.
Researchers study the actions of various prehensors.

senses.definition: relating to prehension or grasping; capable of grasping.
examples: The prehensory limb evolved for better grasping.
This device provides prehensory feedback to users.

senses.definition: situated or occurring before the liver in the body, particularly in relation to blood flow or anatomy.
examples: Prehepatic conditions affect how blood enters the liver.
The prehepatic phase of digestion is when bile is produced.

senses.definition: a Latin-derived term used in medical contexts to refer to prehepatic conditions or classifications.
examples: Diagnosis noted prehepaticus factors in the chart.

senses.definition: occurring before the heroic or epic stage; not yet heroic in narrative or action.
examples: The preheroic tales set the stage for legend.
In preheroic times, communities faced dangers differently.

senses.definition: an older or variant spelling of hesitancy; hesitation before action.
examples: His prehesitancy delayed the decision until it was too late.

senses.definition: to hesitate before acting; to delay.
examples: To avoid mistakes, he prehesitated before answering.
She tends to prehesitate when faced with ambiguity.

senses.definition: past tense of prehesitate; acted with hesitation.
examples: He prehesitated before signing the contract.

senses.definition: present participle of prehesitate; hesitant in action.
examples: She was prehesitating at the edge of the platform.

senses.definition: the act or moment of hesitating before acting; hesitation before a choice.
examples: There was a moment of prehesitation before the leap.
His prehesitation slowed the team's progress.

senses.definition: relating to or consisting of a prehexamer; a term used in biochemistry to describe a state before a six-unit assembly.
examples: The prehexameral stage is observed in the early assembly process.

senses.definition: the act of hiring in advance or to preselect candidates; or a person who is considered for hiring early.
examples: The company announced prehiring of applicants who met criteria.

senses.definition: a person who studies prehistory, especially the period before written records.
examples: The archaeologist is a renowned prehistorian.
Prehistorians study ancient tools and cultures.

senses.definition: plural of prehistorian; people who study prehistory.
examples: Several prehistorians presented new findings.
